title: Almost shorted oil this week. The setup looked clean, but the tape didn’t.
author: dogazine4570
selftext:
I almost shorted oil this week because my backtest gave me the kind of setup I usually like. When crude spikes hard on geopolitical news, I usually wait for the first peace or de-escalation headline, then look for the fade. The idea is simple: the first move is usually panic, and if the worst-case scenario starts looking less likely, some of that panic premium should come out.  
May 4 looked like the panic day. Brent closed around $114.44 and WTI around $106.42 after the Iran/Hormuz escalation headlines. A few days later, the headlines started shifting toward a possible U.S.-Iran deal and traffic through Hormuz reopening. Brent had already slid back toward $100. On the chart, that looked like the trade. My old process would have been to wait for a small bounce, start scaling into a short, and use the May 4 spike as the reference point for where the panic move got overextended.  
But this time I didn’t take it right away. The next step for me was checking whether the headline actually changed the risk, or whether price was just pulling back while the event was still alive. That’s where it got messy. My trading bot with real-time trading news was still showing renewed fighting risk, fragile ceasefire language, shipping concerns, and oil shock headlines.   
I also had Polymarket open on the side, and the Hormuz/oil-related odds were still moving around with almost every update. So instead of treating the “possible deal” headline as the all-clear, I started watching whether oil, Polymarket, and the news flow were all confirming the same thing. They weren’t.  
That was the part that stopped me. Brent falling from $114 to around $100 looked like panic premium coming out, but the live information didn’t really confirm that the risk was gone. It felt more like the market was repricing headline by headline. If the trade had been clean, I would have expected the news flow to calm down, Polymarket odds to stop jumping, and oil to keep fading without every new headline putting risk back into the trade. Instead, the whole thing still felt unstable.  
Then May 8 made the hesitation look right. Brent jumped around 3% intraday after renewed U.S.-Iran fighting headlines, then faded from the highs and settled near $101.29. WTI finished around $95.42. I’m not saying the short could never have worked, but the reason I skipped it was that the setup depended on the event calming down, and it hadn’t really calmed down yet.  
TradingNews helped mostly because it kept the headline sequence in one place instead of letting me anchor on one “peace deal” headline. The main takeaway for me was that a backtest can show you what usually happens after a geopolitical spike, but it can’t tell you whether the current event has actually ended.  
Curious how others handle this. When you fade geopolitical oil spikes, do you treat the first de-escalation headline as enough, or do you wait until the news flow and related markets stop reacting too?
